name: Germanium
name_origin: Named after 'Germania', the Latin name for Germany, by its discoverer Clemens Winkler in 1886; the element matched Mendeleev's predicted 'eka-silicon'.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Germanium is rare in Earth's crust at roughly 1.5 ppm. It has no large concentrated ores; commercial supply comes mainly from by-products of zinc refining and from coal fly ash. Major producers include China, Russia, and the United States.
states: trace in coal and coal fly ash

compounds.name: Germanium dioxide
compounds.summary: White solid with high optical refractive index, used in fiber-optic preforms, infrared optics, and as a polymerization catalyst for PET plastics.

compounds.name: Germanium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: Volatile colorless liquid used as the principal precursor for ultra-pure germanium and for chemical-vapor deposition of GeO2 in optical fibers.

compounds.name: Germane
compounds.summary: Colorless pyrophoric gas analogous to silane, used in CVD of germanium thin films for electronics and photovoltaics.

compounds.name: Silicon-germanium alloy
compounds.summary: Semiconductor alloy used in heterojunction bipolar transistors and strained-silicon CMOS to boost speed and efficiency.

compounds.name: Germanium disulfide
compounds.summary: Glass-forming chalcogenide used in infrared-transmitting glasses and in research on solid-state electrolytes.

uses: Optical fibers (GeO2 doping of silica cores)
Infrared optics for night-vision and thermal imaging
Semiconductor devices including SiGe transistors and high-efficiency solar cells
Fluorescent lighting phosphors and specialty alloys
reactivity_summary: Germanium is a hard, brittle, grayish-white metalloid that is stable in air and water at room temperature. It is unaffected by dilute non-oxidizing acids and alkalis but is attacked by hot concentrated sulfuric acid, nitric acid, and molten alkalis. Like silicon, it forms tetravalent covalent compounds; the +2 state is also accessible but less stable.
isotopes_summary: Germanium has five naturally occurring isotopes: 70Ge (~20.5%), 72Ge (~27.4%), 73Ge (~7.8%), 74Ge (~36.5%), and 76Ge (~7.8%). 76Ge is observationally stable but theoretically undergoes double beta decay, and is the basis of major neutrinoless-double-beta-decay experiments such as GERDA and LEGEND.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
